segments.title: Kure waterfront: JS Kaga and the Yamato Museum
segments.summary: The Kure waterfront brings a modern Japanese carrier into view beside the Yamato Museum and the JMSDF museum submarine display.
segments.body: This opening view makes Kure useful as a compact naval-history stop rather than merely a scenic port. Dr. Clarke points out Kaga in the harbour and then turns to the Yamato Museum, whose exterior deliberately evokes Yamato's bow. He also notes the nearby JMSDF museum and its submarine exhibit. The juxtaposition gives visitors a quick visual link between Japan's imperial-era naval memory and the post-war Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force. It is a useful watch point for seeing why Kure remains such a concentrated naval landscape.

evidence.note: The speaker identifies Kaga, the Yamato Museum bow-inspired frontage, and the JMSDF museum submarine.

segments.title: Kure's naval sites beyond the waterfront
segments.summary: A short itinerary note points to Kure's shipyard tour, harbour cruise, and former commander-in-chief headquarters room.
segments.body: The update sketches a naval-history visit that extends beyond the museum façade. Dr. Clarke says the group is about to tour the shipyard and take a harbour cruise, activities that place the port's present working waterfront alongside its historical displays. He also highlights the former commander-in-chief headquarters room as a memorable part of the day. The moment is brief, but it shows how Kure combines museum interpretation, surviving institutional spaces, and an active maritime setting. Viewers planning a naval-history visit can use it as a pointer to the range of sites gathered in one port.

evidence.note: The speaker mentions a shipyard tour, harbour cruise, and the old commander-in-chief headquarters room.
